halfLife.conditions: adults; tablet label states a serum half-life of 1.8–2.0 h. Elimination is described as BIPHASIC; this is the terminal half-life. Collapsing the biphasic curve to one compartment is faithful to the terminal slope but approximates the early shape (see notes).

vd.conditions: adults; apparent volume ~0.12 L/kg (febrile children <11 y ~0.2 L/kg). Treated as apparent V/F under the apparent-volume convention; scaled to absolute litres against the 70 kg reference subject at derivation time.

F.conditions: set to 1 by the apparent-volume convention. Ibuprofen is rapidly and nearly completely absorbed, but the label reports no explicit absolute F%, so the reported volume is used as apparent V/F to avoid double-counting F.

tmax.conditions: peak serum levels attained 1–2 h after oral administration (fasting or immediately before meals; food delays but does not much reduce total absorption); representative point value 1.5 h within that range

fda_tablet_label.title: Ibuprofen Tablet (Dr. Reddy's Laboratories Louisiana) — FDA Structured Product Label, clinical pharmacology (absorption, half-life, dose linearity)

fda_pk_label.title: Ibuprofen Oral Suspension (Perrigo) — FDA Structured Product Label, clinical pharmacology (volume of distribution, protein binding)

notes: Curated from two FDA Structured Product Labels for ibuprofen (Dr. Reddy's immediate-release tablet for absorption/half-life/dose-linearity; Perrigo oral suspension for volume of distribution and protein binding) — the PK is systemic and route-independent, so combining the two labels is defensible; each parameter's conditions name its source label. ke is taken from the terminal half-life (~2.0 h). TWO honesty caveats matter here. (1) The label explicitly calls the plasma elimination BIPHASIC (a fast distribution phase then the ~2 h terminal phase); v1 is strictly one-compartment, so this drug is collapsed to monophasic. The plotted terminal slope matches the label, but the early post-dose shape is an approximation — the true early concentration falls faster than a single-compartment fit. (2) Dose-proportionality holds for SINGLE doses up to 800 mg (label: linear AUC-vs-dose up to 800 mg); above 800 mg AUC rises less than proportionally because protein binding is saturable (>99% bound at 20 µg/mL, nonlinear above) and metabolism/clearance shift. Within the therapeutic single-dose range (≤800 mg) elimination is first-order and identical repeated doses superpose, so linear: true; the entry should not be used to extrapolate to supratherapeutic doses. Vd is treated as apparent V/F under the apparent-volume convention (F = 1) since the label gives no explicit absolute bioavailability. Educational illustration only — not for dosing.
